speaker: interviewer
text: Rose Dora Presser, tape number two. Rose, can you tell me a little bit more about the circumstances around your marriage to Bernard? And the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding, my sister-- my sister-in-law made my party. It was at her place. Yes. My husband's aunts made a beautiful cakes. And, you know, we have a few of the drinks, was very nice. And I got flowers. And it was nice. It was quite a wedding. [LAUGHS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel rushed to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. They ask me about this when I get-- [INAUDIBLE] asked me did somebody to tell you? They told you to get married? I-- I say, no I wanted to get married. No, I-- nobody can rush me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you pregnant at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not now. Later I used to be pregnant, yeah. I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a few months after the marriage. I don't remember the date. But I went to my doctor that I used to go to before-- a lady doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Fennerge, I remember the name. And she said, I can make you nothing because I know you so well. And you-- when I make you the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I make the pregnancy out, you never will have children. You know, and I can't do this for your family. I know your auntie. I know-- but I said, listen. It's war. What can happen tomorrow, we don't know. She said, sorry. I come and I go to the Polish doctor. Was a very charming lady, and she make me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your health after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good-- perfect. Yes. And I have even twins. It was twins when she made me-- she said, oh God. I'll charge you double because-- she was a very nice lady. Yes, it was all right. And I met this-- my doctor in Auschwitz. And she-- and I tell her, you see, doctor, what can happen-- where we are now? What I would do with my child-- She said, sorry, darling, but I couldn't do nothing for you. It [? happened, yes. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your husband Bernard's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --work. Can you clarify where he was working for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working in the [INAUDIBLE] fabric-- in Schindler place-- night and day-- one week a night, one week daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did-- and what was he telling you about working with Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was quite happy because he knows these people. It happened when he came over there-- that he knows the-- the people who run. This engineer, he was Polish. He was before the war over there. And he was coming over there because he worked in some technical bureau. And he was-- delivered some things over there. And they treat him very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his job in Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he didn't work with the-- in the oven, you know, with the [INAUDIBLE] only. He was working on the-- when they make the tools, you know these things. Yes, he was very good to him, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he try to get you a job in Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After two months, we went-- first we went to Plaszow. I went first to Plaszow--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: From ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm already in the ghetto. I don't know. And later on, after two months, I went to Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before you tell me about Schindler, maybe you could talk to me a little bit about Plaszow camp. What did you-- what was there at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow started-- they built Plaszow, you know, I come one of the first people to Plaszow. And I take only a little wooden case. And I go to Plaszow. First of-- I go first, later he come. There was not much happen over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in your little case?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe some panties-- something. There were not many things you can take-- as much as you can keep in your hand. Everything we left in-- whatever we didn't-- we haven't got, but we leave everything in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you some identification number at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the commandant there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once only, he come. Thank God I did not have contact with him. But once he come to the barrack. And he was taking gold and, you know, whatever you had-- money. You know, and he told me to undress. And I undressed. He take my my wedding ring and something that I have-- I get from him, [INAUDIBLE]-- once he gave me some-- I don't know-- four leaves--

speaker: interviewer
text: Clover.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clover, yes. He gave me for my birthday. And he took this and something else. And then he-- I undressed and he tell me to dress. He can shoot me just like this. Because you can-- when they shot somebody, they say, what's happening? What has he done? They just past the street and he was standing in the balcony. And nothing could happen. He just shot to finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any job or assignment in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work was going on around you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they built the-- the Plaszow, built the barracks-- you know, and the kitchen was. And doctor was over there already. [INAUDIBLE] people would come if they were sick or something. But it was nothing special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any of your family with you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my sister-in-laws and my-- my mother-in-laws too, and my brother. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with them? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We saw each other. Yes, yes. Was-- in the evening, we see-- I should work somewhere but I don't remember where I was working. I was only two months maybe over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you leave Plaszow? How did you get out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We go-- he-- he was working for Schindler, my husband. And we follow him. We went over there to Zablocie. And we lived over there, and we stay over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for your husband to get you a job with Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very hard. Yes. Everybody wants to go over there to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the other family members at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they go-- all of them, they go with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law, my sister-in-law, my brother-in-law and my brother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you hear the news that you got the job at Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean when? One day I had the [INAUDIBLE]. When exactly, I don't remember. After two months, when I was in Plaszow it's happened, no longer. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your first contact with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he come, he come in the morning to the factory. When I-- when he walked, you-- we felt it-- his perfume. The hall was very big, but you go, oh, the director is coming. We could feel when he come. He was always charming-- very nice. Every woman like him. He likes women too, but [LAUGHS] what can he do? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of work were you doing for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was helping with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] measuring some-- some object, what we do now-- for the-- for some army things. I don't know what we call it-- measured this, you know? As a matter of fact, not much I was doing. He was working-- my husband was working hard. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living in the time you were working for Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have the barracks over there. They build the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the factory-- next-- not exactly, but very near the factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the beginning, maybe 500. But later, thousands of people was living over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of your family was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, five-- five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me all their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], you see, my husband's sister, [? Manek, ?] her brother-- her husband, my mother-in-law-- sorry I put her at third place-- [? Francisca ?] [INAUDIBLE] and my brother, four. Four people of the family--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph. [? Yusef, ?] yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you getting any news from outside of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always. Always. We-- we know everything, what happened. What-- the Polish people came every day. We were with Polish people. And they bring us some food because we give them the money. And we get soup twice a day. It was very good. But still, want to have something to eat-- and piece of meat or something they brought for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Schindler paying you well?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Schindler pay you money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, nothing. Nothing. He gave us-- we get a good soup, you know. And we have been treated very well. That's it. No, we never get the money. We sell everything-- whatever we-- my sister-in-law come, I think, or [INAUDIBLE] Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were you able to buy things from the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We get--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. He'll get that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get the money. And I have things in my friend's place-- clothes, you know, and they send-- sell this. And they brought me the-- the food. You know, and they sell these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were-- there were Poles working. Were there working alongside the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband was working with Polish people, yes. They don't believe that he is a Jew, because when he has the-- you know, he worked like that working. And he never think that they are--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relationship between the Polish workers and the Jewish workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good, very good was the relationship. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sorry, what-- what was-- what was the work at the factory? I know you told me you were calibrating--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --munition, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Mhm. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where this munition was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I didn't. To the other factory, where they made a different part. I don't know. I didn't know even what it was. You know. When they make a pots and pans, you know, [? enamel ?] things was a big-- Jewish people have been working over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the pots and pans going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were selling on the black market. And Schindler made money on it-- not on the-- what he worked for the army. He make-- he make this for army, that he can get material to make these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Business was very good. In Poland, it was very good. He made money. Yes, because he-- he paid the people-- that we had been treated properly. You know. No SS men is allowed to come to factory. You know. And no-- nobody-- even the Jewish policemen, they-- they-- because we have a few over there too. But they haven't been allowed to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any visit from the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS look at us over there. We have the SS men looking for us and they search everybody. I never saw him. Maybe some special appell maybe. But they have been very good because he paid them. He had drinks all the time. They had everything. He paid them, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- I imagine that the people working must have grown very close to one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We worked very well together. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the feeling of being in the-- living in the barracks near the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we used to-- we used to cook. Think what we would cook when-- for-- when the war finished. What would we do? You know, and we talked. And they-- we didn't play nothing because we have nothing, because some people worked at nighttime, some people worked at daytime. You know about these shifts?

speaker: interviewer
text: The different shifts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But they were really lovely people. Very nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to pass the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we haven't got much time because we go every day to work. It was not Sunday, Saturday-- you know, never. It was a mill over there going, and it was a very noisy next to our camp. Next to. And, you know, for the beginning, you can get crazy. Later, we didn't hear this. And when it stopped in Christmas [? or some-- ?] it was terrible. You know, suddenly it was so quiet. And it was such a noise, such a strong noise was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any entertainment in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Some girls sang, and-- no, we not have a dancer, nothing like this. But you know, you singing and we talk and nothing. Men talk, women talk. Men were on this side. Women this side. There were very nice people who live in my barrack. Very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone in particular that you were very close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't think so. One girl was really nice-- [? Raina. ?] [? Raina. ?] She was younger. But she was charming-- lovely girl. She's in America now. I read about her in the book. In the-- book. I'll show you the book later-- was how she lived. And she was a lovely girl. There were so many people, you know. I lived with anybody. I haven't got any problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how long the war would last at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- how. [LAUGHS] I think it was forever. It was like this. You never believe that it's finished. It looks like it would never finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you feel like it might be ending?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Don't go there yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you went to Auschwitz. When did-- when did you go to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from-- when?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly the date-- I can't tell you the date. But from Schindler, we went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of Schindler's workers went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many ladies? I can't tell you that either because I don't remember. Maybe 200-- maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it possible--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not 1,000. Maybe 500 because it was again 1,000-- yes, because they come-- the people who never worked in Schindler's place, they come from Plaszow. They were lucky. They pay, you know, to do anything to get in this list, because they believe it-- that this is the list, and they-- they want to go. And then maybe, it was-- together it was 1,000 again because in the meantime, it was less. But later, it was again 1,000. But the men went to Gross-Rosen. Women went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it possible if Schindler was paying the SS to leave you alone-- how is it possible that they came to take you away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because of the-- because the front was coming. You know? And they should liquidate this factory. And ran away. And he-- he tried to this because-- we know that something is going on. And we went to-- to Czechoslovakia-- to Auschwitz first, only for a few weeks. And later we go to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe to me what you saw? Your first sight at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This was very-- very bad thing. You know, we come in the night time-- dogs and night and Germans and screaming-- you know, like a special treatment that you get when you comes straight away. Later, they took us to the barracks. Was shock too-- terrible barrack-- terrible. Very-- no. Before we go to the barracks, we go through this whole-- they take everything what we have. We have a lovely-- I was wearing the [NON-ENGLISH], how do you call this-- this-- this strap thing-- how-- frock, but it's straps. And I have a white collar here. You know, we have-- we look very-- very good from Schindler when we come. And I have a very good pair of shoes. And we have food, because we get-- in the last moment-- we get food from Switzerland-- milk, and the-- little tins of things we bring with us. We didn't eat. We kept it. But they take everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. The shoes what was the best for me. He bought me these German shoes-- little ones, very nice strong shoes because we don't know where we are going. He bought one pair for him and one pair for me. And they take everything. Everything. I only take [? bread ?] in my hand and my husband's picture and my mother's picture. And she wants it. I said, no, I don't it-- the girl who, Jewish-- Jewish girl. And we have things packed in paper-- newspaper. They have been reading the paper and cutting our hair. And they cut your ears because they want to know what is in the world. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- you didn't know where were going. Are you going to the-- to the place where was the chimney was burning or are you going down to the showers? Because it was next to the oven. You know, and when we went in, it was not water coming, because there were two drops of water comes but the shower. But we have been lucky when we go out. They put us outside. It was snowing without nothing. And I was crying and-- and laughing. You know. We're old. You're younger. I said, when I want to maybe have children, when I wash them, I put them outside to dry. Because-- this was-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you a tattoo in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not in Auschwitz. It was, I think, in Brunnlitz. They give us tattoo. My husband still has it, but I haven't got a tattoo. Yes, you have one. I haven't got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sucked it, because maybe we run away. Maybe this-- maybe-- he made me suck-- because I was afraid. I don't think [INAUDIBLE]. You know, and he makes this very-- not so strong. You know, and-- but I suck it. But I don't run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number they gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a number. It was a concentration lager, K-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: K-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: K-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: With no numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No numbers. Numbers they gave the people from Auschwitz, I think, who stayed permanently, I think so. Some of them had--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just want to go back to Schindler's factory. You said the men went to Gross-Rosen and you went to Auschwitz. Did they tell you where you were going when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Schindler told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say when--?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you have some money or some jewelry? Give it to me. I'll give you-- back in-- in Brunnlitz. First of all, we haven't got-- you know? I had one ring or something I kept in my mouth when I was in Auschwitz, and we go through with this. But I-- I-- I believed him always. But this time I couldn't believe him. I say-- I-- he say trust-- he say, trust me. But we didn't give him nothing. And when we come to Czechoslovakia, he say, no Oskar told you, you can trust me. We are here. But it was true. But I couldn't believe it, that we go through Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you about where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: He say you go to Auschwitz and you will be not ago-- long over there. You go to Brunnlitz. But I can't believe it in this. No, it's-- but-- but when you hear about Auschwitz, you hear-- don't believe it. But the Germans-- when you're on the list, [NON-ENGLISH], when you're on the list, you are all right. They-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about Auschwitz before you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I know that the people don't go out from over there-- only through the chimney. I heard about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you hear about this? From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From people-- from people. Somebody ran away. Somebody came back. You know. One died [? to ?] [? get ?] [? come ?] man who ran from-- not from Auschwitz-- from Birkenau, I think. Birkenau was in Auschwitz too. From-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from some place when they-- he ran away. It was a terrible place. And he come to my neighbor when I was living there and he say how he ran away. And-- and you heard something-- somebody said this. Some-- you heard people talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your husband was going to Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were separated? What did you say to each other when you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we say to each other? That we hope we see each other again. It was not so easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you would see him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe it. You know? I tried to believe it. But I-- but I couldn't. But I was-- [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I-- I can't believe it, that we go through this. [INAUDIBLE] was all right, but that's it. We want to run away this time. We want to run away. Friend of his from the factory-- Polish people that he met-- they want to hide us in-- in the place. But his wife should notice and this and that. We don't like this very much. You know, we haven't been so-- [SIGHS] We don't know-- we couldn't really. He wants to build something for us to hide us. But we haven't got the courage to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who left first? Who left the factory first-- the men or the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the same time, we left the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was Schindler while you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember him this moment, when we were leaving. Maybe he was over there. I spoke with him. But I don't know when. It was one day he spoke with me. But in the last moment, you know, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say to you when you spoke to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we would be for sure that I can trust him. That we would be for sure in Brunnlitz. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did he say you would be at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said for-- he-- he told us not very long. Only, you know, is the time we were supposed to be-- two weeks. My husband say we had been six weeks. But I don't-- I was sure it was two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any work? Did you do any work at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was terrible. You-- you don't know when it's day and when it's night. They bring food in the nighttime-- 3 o'clock, 2 o'clock. You are not allowed to go out. You know, and-- it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone you knew there from Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to the toilet. You know. And I went. They gave us the shoes only-- like, a wooden things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clogs. And I walked. Was terrible road. And I was not allowed to, but I didn't know. It was empty everywhere. And I walked. They tell me over there, is the toilet far away? And suddenly, I hear the voice-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I look. Nobody around. Who is it? A ghost? Ghost or something? And it-- it-- but she came out and it was a lady who know me from Krakow. You know, from the-- her father was working in a cemetery. We came every week to the cemetery. My mother was over there. My auntie and my father. And she knows-- she was a daughter of the man who was over there, working, on the cemetery. And she recognized me. How she recognized me-- I looked-- cut hair completely. And frock, they give me from some French girl-- so short, without panties, without nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember the name. But she's here anyway. I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Did Schindler's Jews stay together in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have been together. Yes, on the list-- there was a list.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you treated by the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- we have-- it was some Greek girls in this same barrack where we were. And they had been like little birds, [INAUDIBLE], small. And-- and they take them every day to-- to look how they look. And when they don't look good, they go to the oven. And they-- I say, what are they doing? They put the beetroot on her. I said, what are they crazy? What are they doing? They put beetroot to make good. You know. And it was cold over there. They are not-- the climate killed him. They were coughing. And they-- every day they gone. The selection-- every day they have selection. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you look?

speaker: interviewee
text: We looked very well because we have good-- you know, good-- very well. They took blood from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just-- can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They come and they take all of us to give-- we didn't know what it is. We come over there. They-- they-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll will stop here. And you tell me about the--
